providerContext.label: Specialty anchor available
providerContext.note: Anchorage has the strongest specialty depth and referral coordination capacity in the state, so v1 treats it as the natural reading and scheduling hub.

broadbandContext.label: Reliable connected workflows
broadbandContext.note: Urban connectivity makes image transfer, AI support, and centralized routing materially easier than in frontier regions.

providerContext.label: Regional primary care network
providerContext.note: Primary care coverage is present, but retinal specialty access still depends on coordinated referral pathways and selective hub support.

broadbandContext.label: Mostly connected with fragile edges
broadbandContext.note: Core corridors are workable for fundus-first deployment, but outlying facilities still need staged connectivity support.

providerContext.label: Regional anchor with sparse spokes
providerContext.note: The Fairbanks-area delivery system can anchor a rollout, but surrounding communities still rely on routed referral management.

broadbandContext.label: Mixed reliability outside core hubs
broadbandContext.note: Connected workflows are feasible in the interior hub, though smaller remote sites still face transfer and support constraints.

providerContext.label: Growing primary care base
providerContext.note: A growing clinic footprint supports earlier deployment, while specialty follow-up still benefits from Anchorage coordination.

broadbandContext.label: Connected corridor advantages
broadbandContext.note: Road-system connectivity and population concentration support faster implementation than in frontier regions.

providerContext.label: Sparse workforce coverage
providerContext.note: Primary care coverage is thin and specialty referral logistics are difficult, pushing this region toward infrastructure-first sequencing.

broadbandContext.label: Fragile remote connectivity
broadbandContext.note: Remote connectivity and image-transfer reliability are variable enough that workflow support should precede broad scale-out.

providerContext.label: Distributed network with referral dependence
providerContext.note: A distributed coastal network can support rollout, but consistent referral routing is still necessary across island and marine geographies.

broadbandContext.label: Mixed marine and island connectivity
broadbandContext.note: Several communities are workable for connected imaging, but the region still needs careful workflow design around geography and transport.

providerContext.label: Frontier access constraints
providerContext.note: Primary care access is constrained across a dispersed frontier geography, so device deployment must be paired with stronger referral and implementation support.

broadbandContext.label: Patchy remote connectivity
broadbandContext.note: Connectivity is workable in selected sites, but a region-wide rollout would outrun the current transfer and support environment.

sourceNotes.name: Alaska Health Data Geographic Definitions
sourceNotes.summary: Official Alaska Department of Health public health region definitions used to roll borough and census-area data into the seven policy regions.

sourceNotes.name: ACS 5-Year County Population
sourceNotes.summary: County-level ACS population counts used as the public population baseline for each Alaska public health region.

sourceNotes.name: CDC PLACES County Data
sourceNotes.summary: County-level adult prevalence estimates for diabetes and access barriers used to construct the Alaska regional need baseline.

sourceNotes.name: ADA 2026 Retinopathy Guidance
sourceNotes.summary: Guidance anchor for diabetic retinopathy screening strategy, including the strength of fundus-first workflows and autonomous AI screening pathways.

sourceNotes.name: LA County Teleretinal Implementation
sourceNotes.summary: Large-scale public health implementation study used to inform throughput, referral completion, and wait-time improvement assumptions.

sourceNotes.name: I-SITE Telemedicine Sustainability Study
sourceNotes.summary: Teleophthalmology implementation evidence used to ground screening uptake and sustained delivery assumptions.

sourceNotes.name: Telehealth Screening Economic Model
sourceNotes.summary: Economic modeling reference used to bound reimbursement, avoided cost, and ROI assumptions for tele-retinal screening.

sourceNotes.name: Primary Care Retinal Disease Detection Study
sourceNotes.summary: Supports the fundus-first public default while treating OCT as an adjunct or advanced scenario rather than the main production baseline.

sourceNotes.name: Nursing-Teleophthalmology Integration Study
sourceNotes.summary: Used to support the trained MA or RN staffing model and assumptions about reduced unnecessary specialist referrals.

sourceNotes.name: Teleophthalmology Cost Analysis
sourceNotes.summary: Billing-code and cost-analysis reference used to keep program economics within a realistic range for the public ROI model.

sourceNotes.name: Primary Care Teleophthalmology Access Evaluation
sourceNotes.summary: Primary-care implementation evidence used to calibrate funded value per screen and practical rollout performance.

sourceNotes.name: Prevention of Blindness by Diabetic Retinopathy Screening
sourceNotes.summary: Classic quantitative screening assessment used here only as a conservative anchor for the proposition that earlier identification can materially reduce preventable vision loss.

sourceNotes.name: Laser Photocoagulation for Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy
sourceNotes.summary: Treatment review used to support the idea that moving patients into timely retinal management can reduce severe visual loss and late-stage rescue burden.

sourceNotes.name: Impact of Diabetic Retinopathy on Quality of Life
sourceNotes.summary: Quality-of-life study used to justify explicit QALY-style and well-being assumptions for progressive vision loss and blindness.

sourceNotes.name: Annual Economic Burden of Vision Loss in the United States
sourceNotes.summary: Used to bound productivity and broader economic burden assumptions tied to severe vision loss in working-age and older adults.

sourceNotes.name: Broader Economic Value of Diabetic Macular Edema Treatment
sourceNotes.summary: Economic value reference used to keep productivity and late-stage consequence assumptions tied to the real burden of avoidable retinal disease.

sourceNotes.name: WHO Blindness and Vision Impairment Fact Sheet
sourceNotes.summary: World Health Organization framing for the functional, social, and health burden of avoidable vision impairment and blindness.

sourceNotes.name: FCC Broadband Data Collection
sourceNotes.summary: Connectivity reference used to justify explicit broadband and cybersecurity cost and readiness assumptions for rural implementation.
